questions.prompt: What's the difference between DNSSEC and DoH?

choices.text: Different problems, complementary solutions. DNSSEC adds cryptographic signatures to DNS records so resolvers can verify answers are authentic — defeats spoofing / cache poisoning / forged responses. But DNSSEC doesn't encrypt; an eavesdropper still sees the queries and answers in plaintext on the wire. DoH (DNS over HTTPS) encrypts the channel between client and resolver — defeats eavesdropping and on-path tampering, but doesn't verify the answer's authenticity end-to-end (you trust your DoH resolver). Together: DNSSEC verifies the answer hasn't been forged; DoH hides what you asked from passive observers. Modern best practice is to enable both: DoH on the client side (Firefox, Chrome, OS), DNSSEC on the authoritative side for your domains. Each addresses one property; combined they give you authenticated + confidential DNS.

questions.explanation: DNSSEC = signed records (authenticity); DoH = encrypted channel (confidentiality). Complementary.

questions.prompt: Your DNSSEC keys expire over the weekend; resolvers return SERVFAIL. Site appears down. Recovery?

choices.text: Re-sign with current keys / rotate to valid ones IMMEDIATELY — most DNS providers offer one-click re-sign. Alternative emergency: temporarily disable DNSSEC (un-sign the zone) so resolvers fall back to unsigned answers; this restores access while you fix the keys properly. Long-term: monitor key expiry and rotate well before expiration; many providers handle this automatically (Cloudflare, Route 53 with DNSSEC managed mode). DNSSEC operational discipline matters — broken signatures cause outages that look like 'site down' to validating resolvers. Set up monitoring: tools like DNSCheck or Hardenize alert on signature problems. Lesson learned: don't enable DNSSEC unless you trust your DNS provider's automation or have alerting on key health. A small percentage of resolvers strictly validate DNSSEC; that percentage is enough to ruin a Monday morning.

questions.explanation: Re-sign or temporarily disable DNSSEC. Monitor key expiry; use managed DNSSEC where possible.
